volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Trinummus (Section 4.2.973b - Section 4.3.1059b)

translation.label: Scaife English translation
translation.citation: Trinummus: The Three Pieces of Money

sections.label: Section 4.2.973b
sections.original: Nequiquam hercle es , nam nihil auri fero .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you are so to no purpose , for I carry [*] no gold . Right cleverly were you down upon me , at the very nick of time .

sections.label: Section 4.2.975
sections.original: postquam ego me aurum ferre dixi , post tu factu'sfactus es Charmides ;
sections.translation: After I said that I was bringing the gold , that instant you became Charmides . Before I made mention of the gold , you were not he . It won ’ t do . Just , therefore , in such manner as you Charmidised yourself , do you again un-Charmidise yourself .

sections.label: Section 4.2.978
sections.original: Quis ego sum igitur , siquidem is non sum qui sum ?
sections.translation: Who am I , then , if in fact I am not he who I really am ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.978b
sections.original: Quid id ad me attinet ?
sections.translation: What matters that to me ? So long as you are not he whom I do not choose you to be , you may be who you like , for what I care .

sections.label: Section 4.2.980
sections.original: prius non is eras quí eras : nunc is factu'sfactus es qui tum non eras .
sections.translation: Just now , you were not he who you were , now you are become he who then you were not .

sections.label: Section 4.2.981
sections.original: Age si quid agis .
sections.translation: Come , despatch , if you are going to do it .

sections.label: Section 4.2.981b
sections.original: Quid ego agam ?
sections.translation: What am I to do ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.981c
sections.original: Aurum redde .
sections.translation: Give me back the gold .

sections.label: Section 4.2.981d
sections.original: Dormitas , senex .
sections.translation: You are dreaming , old gentleman .

sections.label: Section 4.2.982
sections.original: Fassu'sfassus es Charmidem dedisse aurum tibi .
sections.translation: Did you own that Charmides delivered the gold to you ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.982b
sections.original: Scriptum quidem .
sections.translation: Yes — in writing [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.983
sections.original: Properas an non properas ire actutum ab his regionibus ,
sections.translation: Are you making haste or not , you night-robber , to be off with all speed this very instant from this neighbourhood , before I order you to be soundly cudgelled on the spot ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.985
sections.original: Quam ob rem ?
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.985b
sections.original: Quia illum quem ementitus es , ego sum ipsus Charmides ,
sections.translation: Because I am that self-same Charmides about whom you have been thus lying , and who you said gave the letters to you .

sections.label: Section 4.2.986b
sections.original: Eho , quaeso , an tu is es ?
sections.translation: How now ; prithee , are you really he ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.987
sections.original: Is enim vero sum .
sections.translation: I really am he .

sections.label: Section 4.2.987b
sections.original: Ain tu tandem ? is ipsusne es ?
sections.translation: Say you so , pray ? Are you really he himself ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.987c
sections.original: Aio .
sections.translation: I do say so .

sections.label: Section 4.2.987d
sections.original: Ipsus es ?
sections.translation: Are you his own self ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.988
sections.original: Ipsus , inquam , Charmides sum .
sections.translation: His own self , I say . I am Charmides .

sections.label: Section 4.2.988b
sections.original: Ergo ipsusne es ?
sections.translation: And are you then his own self ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.988c
sections.original: Ipsissimus .
sections.translation: His own very self . Begone hence out of my sight .

sections.label: Section 4.2.989b
sections.original: Enim véro serio , quoniam advenis —
sections.translation: Since you really have made your appearance here thus late ,

sections.label: Section 4.2.990
sections.original: vapulabis meo arbitratu ét novorum aedilium .
sections.translation: you shall be beaten both at my own award [*] and that of the new Aediles .

sections.label: Section 4.2.991
sections.original: At etiam maledicis ?
sections.translation: And are you abusing me as well ?

sections.label: Section 4.2.991b
sections.original: Immo , salvos quandoquidem advenis —
sections.translation: Yes ; seeing that you have arrived in safety [*] , may the Gods confound me , if I care a straw for you , had you perished first . I have received the money for this job ; you , I devote to bad luck . But who you are , or who you are not , I care not one jot .

sections.label: Section 4.2.995
sections.original: ibo , ad illúm renuntiabo qui mihi tris nummos dedit ,
sections.translation: I ’ ll go and carry word [*] to him who gave me the three pieces , that he may know that he has thrown them away . I ’ m off . Live with a curse , and fare you ill ; may all the Gods confound you , Charmides , for coming from abroad [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.2.998
sections.original: Postquam illic hinc ábiit , post loquendi libere
sections.translation: Since this fellow has gone , at last a time and opportunity seem to have arrived for speaking out without restraint .

sections.label: Section 4.2.1000
sections.original: iam dudum meum ille pectus pnngit aculeus ,
sections.translation: Already does this sting pierce my breast — what business he could have before my house ? For these letters summon apprehensions into my heart ; those thousand pieces , too — what purpose they were to serve . I ’ faith , a bell [*] is never rung for no purpose ;

sections.label: Section 4.2.1005
sections.original: nisi qui illud tractat aut movet , mutumst , tacet .
sections.translation: unless some one handles it or moves it , ’ tis mute , ’ tis dumb . But who is this , that is beginning to run this way along the street ? I should like to observe what he is about . I ’ ll step aside this way .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1008
sections.original: Stasime , fac te propere celerem , recipe te ad dominum domum ,
sections.translation: Stasimus , make you haste with all speed ; away with you to your master ’ s house , lest on a sudden , through your folly , fears should arise for your shoulder-blades [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1010
sections.original: adde gradum , adpropera . iam dudum factumst , cum abiisti domo .
sections.translation: Quicken your pace , make haste ; ’ tis now a long while since you left the house . If you shall be absent when inquired after by your master , take you care , please , that the smacks of the bull ’ s-hide [*] don ’ t clatter thick upon you . Don ’ t you cease running . See now , Stasimus , what a worthless fellow you are ; and isn ’ t it the fact that you have forgotten your ring [*] at the liquor- shop [*] after you have been washing your throat with warm drink ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.1015
sections.original: recipe te et recurre petere re recenti .
sections.translation: Turn about , and run back now , to seek it , while the thing has but just happened .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1015b
sections.original: Huic , quisquis est ,
sections.translation: Whoever he is , his throat is his taskmaster [*] ; that teaches this fellow the art of running .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1017
sections.original: Quid , homo nihili , non pudet te ? tribusne te poteriis
sections.translation: What , good-for-nothing fellow , are you not ashamed of yourself ? having lost your memory after only three cups ? And really , because you were there drinking together with such honest fellows , who could keep their hands off [*] the property of another without difficulty ;

sections.label: Section 4.3.1020
sections.original: Truthus fuit , Cerconicus , Crinnus , Cercobulus , Collabus ,
sections.translation: Chiruchus was there , Cerconicus , Crimnus , Cricolabus , Collabus [*] , whipped-necks [*] , whipped-legs , iron-rubbers , whipped-knaves .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1022
sections.original: inter eosne homines condalium te redipisci postulas ?
sections.translation: — is it among such men that you expect you may recover your ring ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.1023
sections.original: quorum eorum únus surrupuit currenti cursori solum .
sections.translation: By my faith , any one of these could steal the sole of his shoe from a running footman [*] .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1024
sections.original: Ita me di ament , graphicum furem .
sections.translation: So may the Gods love me , a finished thief .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1024b
sections.original: Quid ego quod periit petam ?
sections.translation: Why should I go seek what is gone for ever ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.1025
sections.original: nisi etiam laborem ad damnum ápponam epithecam insuper .
sections.translation: Unless I would bestow my pains , too , by way of addition over and above to my loss . Why , then , don ’ t you consider that what is gone is gone ? Tack about , then [*] . Betake yourself back to your master .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1027b
sections.original: Non fugitivost hic homo , commeminit domi .
sections.translation: This fellow is no runaway ; he remembers his home .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1028
sections.original: Vtinam veteres homin um mor es , veteres parsimoniae
sections.translation: I wish that the old-fashioned ways of old-fashioned days , and the old-fashioned thriftiness , were in greater esteem here , rather than these bad ways .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1030
sections.original: Di immortales , basilica hic quidem facinora inceptat loqui .
sections.translation: Immortal Gods ! this man really is beginning to talk of noble doings ! He longs for the old-fashioned ways ; know that he loves the old-fashioned ways , after the fashion of our forefathers .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1032
sections.original: Nam nunc mores nihili faciunt quod licet , nisi quod lubet :
sections.translation: For , now-a-days , men ’ s manners reckon of no value what is proper , except what is agreable . Ambition now is sanctioned by usage , and is free from the laws . By usage , people have the license to throw away their shields , and to run away from the enemy .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1035
sections.original: petere honorem pro flagitio more fit .
sections.translation: To seek honor thereby in place of disgrace is the usage .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1035b
sections.original: Morem improbum .
sections.translation: A shameless usage .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1036
sections.original: Strenuiores praeterire more fit .
sections.translation: Now-a-days , ’ tis the usage to neglect the brave .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1036b
sections.original: Nequam quidem .
sections.translation: Aye , ’ tis really shocking .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1037
sections.original: Mores leges perduxerunt iam in potestatem suam ,
sections.translation: The public manners have now got the laws in their power ; to them they are more submissive than are parents to their children [*] . In their misery , these laws are even hung up [*] against the wall with iron nails ,

sections.label: Section 4.3.1040
sections.original: ubi malos mores adfigi nimio fuerat aequius .
sections.translation: where it had been much more becoming for bad ways to be fixed up .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1041
sections.original: Lubet adire atque appellare hunc ; verum ausculto perlubens
sections.translation: I ’ d like to go up and accost this person ; but I listen to him with much pleasure , and I ’ m afraid , if I address him , that he may begin to talk on some other subject .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1043
sections.original: Neque istis quicquam lege sanctumst : leges mori serviunt ,
sections.translation: And , for these ways , there is nothing rendered sacred by the law . The laws are subservient to usage ; but these habits are hastening to sweep away both what is sacred and what is public property .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1045
sections.original: Hercle istis malam rem magnam moribus dignumst dari .
sections.translation: By my troth , ’ twere right for some great calamity to befal these bad customs .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1046
sections.original: Nonne hoc publice animum advorti ? nam id genus hominum omnibus
sections.translation: Ought not this state of things to be publicly censured ? For this kind of men are the enemies of all persons , and do an injury to the entire people . By a non-observance of their own honour , they likewise destroy all trust even in those who merit it not ; inasmuch as people form an estimate of the disposition of these from the disposition of those fellows .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1050
sections.original: hoc qui in mentem venerit mihi ? re ipsa modo commonitus sum .
sections.translation: As to how this suggests itself to me , I have by actual experience been lately put in mind of it .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1051
sections.original: si quoi mutuom quid dederis , fit pro proprio perditum :
sections.translation: If you lend [*] a person any money , it becomes lost for any purpose as one ’ s own . When you ask for it back again , you may find a friend made an enemy by your kindness . If you begin to press still further , the option of two things ensues — either you must part with that which you have entrusted , or else you must lose that friend .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1055
sections.original: Meus est hic quidem Stasimus servos .
sections.translation: Surely this is my servant Stasimus ?

sections.label: Section 4.3.1055b
sections.original: Nam ego talentum mutuom
sections.translation: For as to him to whom I lent the talent , I bought myself an enemy with my talent , and sold my friend . But I am too great a simpleton to be attending to public matters rather than ( what ’ s my immediate interest ) obtain safety for my back . I ’ ll go home .

sections.label: Section 4.3.1059b
sections.original: Heus tu , asta ilico . audi .
sections.translation: Hallo , you ! Stop , this instant ! Harkye ; hallo , you !
